title: Fanout Exchanges and True Publish/Subscribe · Inference Brief: Late Subscriber Semantics
summary: Audit, notification, search, and analytics consumers share one queue and steal work from each other; a fanout exchange exists but late or disconnected subscriber behavior is undocumented. Planned artifact: Add and verify the late subscriber semantics increment in fanout subscription topology and lifecycle map. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: State whether late subscribers miss prior events, receive retained queue backlog, or require a stream or database replay path.
Design durable subscription queues with retention, offline backlog, capacity, replay alternatives, and ownership limits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Late Subscriber Semantics · Theory Late Subscriber Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory messaging team handles case 6169 in a quorum queue during a planned rolling maintenance window. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove one subscription queue per responsibility and explicit late-subscriber behavior; preserve one tenant fairness invariant; then change one routing segment and prove the exact destination set. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Late Subscriber Semantics contract for the supplied data.
